records.shortDefinition: A computer or program that receives requests and provides files, data, or actions to another computer called a client.
Hardware and software that accept web requests and return pages, files, or application responses through HTTP.
A device or program—such as a browser—that requests information or actions from a server.
Software that requests web resources, interprets them, and presents an interactive page to a visitor.
A human-readable name used to reach internet services, controlled through registration and DNS rather than by the website files themselves.
The distributed naming system that directs a domain to website, email, verification, and other internet services.
A DNS server that answers questions about names; authoritative nameservers provide the final records for a domain's zone.
A typed instruction in DNS that directs traffic or publishes information for a domain or hostname.
The service and infrastructure that make website files or application responses available to internet visitors.
A website whose deployable pages and assets are prepared ahead of requests instead of requiring server code for every visit.
A distributed network that serves cached website resources from locations closer to visitors.
The request-and-response protocol browsers, servers, and many APIs use to exchange web resources and actions.
The security protocol used by HTTPS to protect data while it travels between a client and server.
An address that identifies a resource by scheme, host, path, and optional query or fragment information.
A response that tells a browser or crawler to request a resource at a different URL.
A layout approach that adapts content and interaction to different screen sizes, zoom levels, input methods, and available space.
A version-control system that records changes to files so work can be compared, reviewed, branched, and restored.
A hosted platform for Git repositories, code review, permissions, documentation, automation, releases, and related project operations.
A project container holding files, revision history, branches, and collaboration records.
A parallel line of repository work used to develop or review changes without immediately altering the main version.
A recorded repository revision that identifies a specific set of changes and its place in project history.
The controlled process that makes a selected version of source available in a hosting environment.
Automated checks and release workflows that turn reviewed source changes into tested, deployable versions.
A defined way for one program to request data or actions from another program.
A credential-like string used by an API to associate a request with a project, quota, or permitted capability.
A delegated authorization framework that lets a user grant limited application access without giving the application the user's password.
Runtime configuration supplied outside application source, commonly used for environment-specific values and secret references.
An event-driven HTTP message one system sends to another when something changes.
A structured text format commonly used to exchange named values, lists, and objects between software systems.
A repeatable process performed by software according to defined triggers, inputs, rules, permissions, and review points.
A client-server protocol that lets AI applications discover approved tools, resources, and prompt templates through defined connections.
A program that exposes a defined set of MCP tools, resources, or prompts to an approved MCP client.
The protocol component inside an AI host that maintains a connection to one MCP server.
An executable function an MCP server advertises for the client to request with defined inputs and outputs.
Contextual data an MCP server makes readable through an addressable resource interface.
Automated retrieval and traversal of web resources to inspect, index, monitor, or analyze publicly reachable content.
An automated client that requests web resources and may follow links according to a defined purpose and policy.
A top-level text file that publishes crawl rules for automated clients following the Robots Exclusion Protocol.
A machine-readable list of canonical URLs a site wants eligible crawlers to discover.
A search system's process of evaluating and storing information about a page for possible retrieval in results.
Google's event-based analytics product for measuring configured website or application activity and reporting it through an interface and APIs.
A defined action or occurrence recorded by an analytics system with approved contextual properties.
A visitor action the business has deliberately defined as meaningful progress toward an outcome.
Google's owner-facing service for monitoring and debugging how a verified website appears and performs in Google Search.
A Google-managed business listing that can appear across Google Search and Maps with approved business information and interactions.
Work that improves how search systems can access, understand, and present useful website information without guaranteeing placement.
SEO focused on helping an eligible business become easier to find, understand, and evaluate in a real local market.
The technical work that helps the right website pages remain accessible, crawlable, indexable, canonical, fast, and correctly connected.
The collection and interpretation of defined data to answer questions about behavior, performance, and business decisions.
Structured information describing a page or resource, including titles, descriptions, canonical references, and social-sharing fields.
The preferred URL identified for a page when the same or closely related content can be reached through multiple URLs.
Machine-readable fields that describe visible entities and content using an agreed vocabulary and supported format.
Software used to create, organize, edit, and publish website content without manually changing every delivered file.
A stored reusable copy that can answer a request faster than recreating or retrieving the original resource again.
How quickly a visitor can understand what a business offers, who it serves, why it matters, and what to do next.
A specific, checkable detail that reduces uncertainty about a business, its offer, its process, or the next step.
The paths customers use to find and begin evaluating a business, service, product, or answer.
A useful next step a customer takes, such as calling, requesting a quote, booking, visiting, buying, or making a qualified inquiry.
A clear invitation that tells a visitor which useful action they can take next.
The underlying task or decision a person is trying to complete when they enter a search query.
A word or phrase used to describe a search topic; actual search queries provide the more precise observed wording.
A company authorized to register domain names and maintain the registrant's account and renewal relationship.
A recoverable copy of website source, content, data, configuration, and other state needed to restore service.
A digital certificate used during TLS to authenticate a website name and establish an encrypted connection.
A website that generates or changes responses using application logic, stored data, user state, or external services.
The markup language that gives a web page its content structure and meaning.
An interface that lets a visitor submit structured information or request an action.
The practice of making websites perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust for people with varied abilities and technologies.
How quickly and reliably a website loads, renders, responds, and remains stable for real visitors.
A link from one page on a website to another page on the same website.
A page designed as the arrival point for a specific audience, campaign, query, offer, or next action.
A page that explains a distinct service, who it is for, the problem it addresses, and the appropriate next action.
A page that provides useful, location-specific information for a real service area, office, shop, or market.
A system for recording changes to files so work can be compared, attributed, combined, and restored.
The process that transforms source files and dependencies into output that can be tested or deployed.
The live environment that serves real visitors or supports real business operations.
A non-production environment used to review and test a release candidate before it reaches real visitors.
A controlled way to restore a known prior state after a release causes unacceptable problems.
An organized system for storing and retrieving structured information used by an application or workflow.
A maintained connection that lets separate systems exchange data, events, or actions.
A specific address and operation exposed by an API for requesting data or performing an action.
The process of verifying that a person, service, or device is the identity it claims to be.
The rules and decisions that determine which resources or actions an authenticated identity may use.
Information or a cryptographic object presented to establish identity or access a protected system.
Authentication that requires evidence from more than one independent factor category.
A defined sequence of actions, decisions, handoffs, and states used to complete a repeatable piece of work.
Repeated observation of a system's availability, behavior, errors, or business signals so failures can be detected.
A person or organization that has shown identifiable interest and may have a relevant business need.
The coordinated process of attracting, informing, capturing, routing, and following up with potential customers.
A lead that meets agreed criteria for relevance, need, authority, timing, location, or commercial fit.
The share of an explicitly defined population that completed an explicitly defined conversion.
A defined quantitative measure used to observe behavior, condition, progress, or outcome.
Vanity metrics are numbers that look impressive without helping a team make better decisions.
Data a business collects through its own direct relationships, properties, and interactions.
A small piece of data a website asks a browser to store and send with later matching requests.
The process and controls used to request, record, honor, and update a person's choices about data use.
Information that identifies, relates to, or can reasonably be connected with a person, depending on context and jurisdiction.
The rules and practices that determine how long data is kept and what happens when it is no longer needed.
A link from a page on another website to a page on the business's website.
An online mention or listing of a business's identifying details on a directory, platform, association, or other site.
The practice of keeping a business's name, address, and phone information accurate and logically consistent across important listings.
A software system that uses an AI model to interpret context, choose steps, and act through bounded tools or workflows.
A machine-learning model trained to process and generate language by predicting likely sequences from context.
Instructions and context supplied to a language model to shape the task and response.
A product or managed environment that provides reusable capabilities other websites, tools, or workflows can run on or connect to.
The organized relationship among a website's pages, URLs, navigation, internal links, templates, and content types.
A consolidated view that presents selected metrics, events, statuses, or alerts for a defined decision or operating task.
A recorded starting state used to compare what changed after an intervention, release, or measurement period.
Human-readable instructions and related files used to build, configure, or operate software.
The complete body of source files and related project material that make up a particular software system.
The controlled point when users, traffic, or operations move from the previous system to the replacement.
A three-digit number in an HTTP response that describes the outcome of a request.
The HTTP response used when the server cannot find the requested resource.
An HTTP redirect status indicating that a resource has moved permanently to another URL.
The time-limited registration of a domain name through an accredited registrar for an identified registrant.
A third-party internet-infrastructure provider whose services include DNS, traffic proxying, content delivery, hosting, security, and data products.
Cloudflare's third-party challenge service for assessing whether a website request appears automated or abusive.
Cloudflare's managed serverless SQL database product, built around SQLite-compatible semantics and application bindings or APIs.
An open-source web-analytics application that reports configured page views and events through dashboards and an API.
An encrypted protocol used for authenticated remote login and other secure network services.
A broad category of computer systems that perform tasks associated with prediction, classification, generation, planning, or decision support.
A Level AA conformance target within the W3C Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2.
Hardware or software that helps a person perceive, navigate, operate, or communicate through a digital interface.
Commercial activity in which the primary buyer is another business or organization rather than an individual consumer.
A page or section that answers recurring user questions in a structured, scannable form.
A network address used to route traffic to or from a device or service.
Information a client sends with a web request describing its software and sometimes device or platform characteristics.
A browser preference signal asking sites not to track the user's activity.
Data a website stores in a browser for state, preferences, sessions, or application behavior.
An analytics record that a page was loaded or displayed under a configured tracking definition.
A server or delivery-platform record describing requests it received and the responses it produced.
The server-side destination that receives and handles a web-form submission.
The practical ability of permitted crawlers to request and traverse a page or website.
A page's technical eligibility to be considered for a search index.
An instruction telling compliant crawlers whether content may be indexed or links followed.
A sequence in which one URL redirects to another URL that redirects again before the final response.
A page with no ordinary internal links pointing to it from the website.
An add-on that extends a website platform or content-management system with additional behavior.
The HTML document title browsers and search systems use to label a page in tabs, bookmarks, and often search results.
A rule set for assigning credit for a conversion or outcome across marketing or customer touchpoints.
Configured collection of website interactions or technical signals for measurement, diagnostics, or attribution.
Transforming data from different sources into consistent names, types, units, and structures.
A group of related pages organized around a central subject and connected through deliberate internal links.
Software whose source is available under a license permitting specified forms of use, inspection, modification, and redistribution.
A permissive open-source license allowing broad reuse while requiring preservation of its copyright and license notice.
Software operated on infrastructure controlled by the user or organization instead of only through a vendor-managed service.
The practice of granting only the access needed for a defined task, system, and period.
A verified website or URL scope represented as a managed property in a search-platform account.
Measurement that connects phone calls to a source, page, campaign, or customer path under a defined configuration.
A stylesheet language that controls how structured web documents are presented across screens, print, speech, and other media.
A non-ionizing medical-imaging method that uses strong magnetic fields, radiofrequency energy, and measured signals to construct detailed images.
A specialized MRI method that estimates changes associated with brain activity through time-varying blood-flow or blood-oxygenation signals.
An equation relating a function of one independent variable to one or more of that function's derivatives.
